EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure aloft moved across Arkansas on the evening of the 9th, touching off light freezing rain and sleet in parts of central and southern Arkansas. An upper trough and an associated cold front moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the evening of May 21st, 2013. Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system affecting the Mid-South. Some storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The first round of storms moved through during the morning hours while a second round affected the region during the afternoon into the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was moving slowly through Eastern Oklahoma into Western Arkansas during the afternoon hours of May 16th. A lone supercell thunderstorm across North Central Texas developed during the late morning hours of May 16th. This storm developed in a strongly sheared environment which allowed the storm to sustain itself. The storm moved south and east towards a strong instability axis which was oriented across Northeast Texas into Northwest Louisiana. With strong heating, strong instability developed and the supercell thunderstorm intensified as it moved south and east. The storm produced several tornado tracks, some of which occurred in Harrison County Texas and other into Southwest Caddo Parish and Northern Desoto Parish. The storm was a prolific hail and wind producer as well with wind gusts near 100 mph south of Shreveport, Louisiana with large fields of hail which turned numerous acres of land completely white.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed along with power poles.  Some structural damage was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system passed by to the northwest of Arkansas on the 25th and 26th. There were numerous reports of hail on the 25th, due to the cold air aloft associated with the low pressure. In many cases, the hail was small but there were great quantities of it. The ground was covered by hail in quite a few places, and hail even piled up on roads in the more extreme cases. After cold air spilled into Arkansas on the back side of the low pressure system, light snow fell in Boone County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot temperatures combined with very high humidity resulted in excessive heat across west central Arkansas. Temperatures climbed into the mid 90s to near 100 and heat index values ranged from 105 to 110 degrees. Very little relief was realized at night with temperatures only falling into the mid 70s to near 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed in Little Rock late on the evening of the 2nd and continued until the wee hours of the 4th. Much of the time, visibility was 1/4 mile or less, though visibility did improve somewhat for a couple of hours on the afternoon of the 3rd. The lowest visibility was zero for four hours during the early morning of the 3rd. More than 30 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport.

A weak area of low pressure aloft moved across Arkansas on the evening of the 9th, touching off light freezing rain and sleet in parts of central and southern Arkansas. Although amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less, the ground was cold from a previous intrusion of Arctic air. As a result, icy patches developed on a number of highways and county roads.

A strong upper level trough across the southern Great Basin ejected out into the Southern Plains during the day on May 21st. Strong convection developed during peak heating across the region and this convection was aided by moderate to strong instability, excessive deep layer shear and mid level lapse rates. Add a frontal boundary to the mix and severe thunderstorms became widespread, some of which produced large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground in Junction City, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged dry spell during the latter part of the summer produced severe (D2) drought conditions across portions of East-Central Arkansas by the beginning of September. However, drought conditions improved by the latter part of the month as several periods of rain occurred from passing cold fronts. Little damage occurred to crops as crops were being harvested at the time. Cattle possibly had a hard time finding adequate water sources as rivers and lakes were at low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions occurred across Lee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over central Oklahoma during the early afternoon hours of the 20th. Very unstable air combined with strong low-level and deep-layer shear resulted in long-lived supercells forming over central Oklahoma and moving eastward into eastern Oklahoma. The storms organized into bow echos and line segments over eastern Oklahoma, from which several other tornadoes developed during the early evening hours. One such tornado moved into northwestern Arkansas dissipating north of Cincinnati in Washington County. Two other tornadoes developed in Benton County. Damaging wind and large hail was also widespread across the region from these storms.

Peak afternoon heating and a cold frontal boundary were all the ingredients needed for strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Sevier County, Arkansas.  These storms produced damaging downburst winds which downed trees and powerlines across portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in and just west of De Queen, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure was located across the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon hours of September 11th. Weak perturbations aloft were moving from east to west under the ridge and provided the lift needed for widely scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours. Storms produced strong wind gusts across Southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were downed along Campbell Road and Hinson Road in the Parkers Chapel Community.  One tree fell through a mobile home causing significant damage.  There were no reports of injuries.

Heat index values of 105 to 110 degrees occurred during the day with temperatures only falling into the mid to upper 70s at night, resulting in little relief from the heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several hundred people were treated for heat-related issues before, during, and after the Arkansas Razorbacks football game in Fayetteville. A couple dozen of those people were transported to the hospital due to the pronounced nature of their symptoms. The temperature in Fayetteville climbed into the mid 90s during the afternoon. This heat combined with the humidity resulted in heat index values in the upper 90s. The Wet-bulb Globe Temperature, another indicator of heat stress, approached 90 during the afternoon. This index takes into account not only the temperature and humidity but also the wind speed, sun angle, and cloud cover.

Low pressure aloft moving northward from Louisiana sparked an increase in thunderstorms in Arkansas on the 2nd. Coverage was greatest over the eastern half of the state. An isolated severe storm and locally heavy rain occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall caused the drought in parts of southeastern Arkansas to worsen briefly at the beginning of October.  However, rain during the first half of the month improved conditions, with severe drought disappearing from the Little Rock County Warning Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of October, extreme drought spread from southwestern Bradley County into the northeastern part of the county, and severe drought covered the rest of the county. By the middle of the month, conditions improved, with both the extreme drought and the severe drought disappearing from the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barns and sheds were damaged or destroyed, a carport was blown away, shingles were blown off roofs, and trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale upper trough sat up over the Upper and Lower Mississippi River Valleys on July 22nd-July 23rd, 2013. Several upper level disturbances rotated around the base of the upper trough. The disturbances set off mesoscale convective complexes during the overnight hours which pushed south during the daytime. The convection would die out during the afternoon hours. This happened on the 22nd and again on the 23rd. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Biggers received 3.5 to 3.75 inches of rain which produced flash flooding. Numerous streets were flooded and impassable as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northwest Arkansas on the morning of the 20th and lingered in the state until the night of the 21st. While the front was making its way slowly across the state, an area of low pressure arrived from the southwest.  The result was heavy rain and flash flooding in the cooler air behind the front. In the warmer air ahead of the front, isolated tornadoes occurred.||Some of the larger rainfall totals for the 48 hours that included the 20th and 21st were:  7.92 inches 3.3 miles west of Carlisle (Lonoke Co.), 7.68 inches at Little Dixie (Woodruff Co.), 7.66 inches at Des Arc (Prairie Co.), 7.37 inches at Keo (Lonoke Co.), 6.71 inches at Georgetown (White Co.), 6.50 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 6.45 inches at Augusta (Woodruff Co.), 6.29 inches at Chenal Valley (Pulaski County), 6.15 inches at DeGray Lake (Clark Co.), 6.12 inches 8.8 miles west of Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), and 6.00 inches 0.5 mile southwest of Beebe (White Co.).  Numerous other stations reported amounts between 4 and 6 inches, especially in eastern, central, and southern parts of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads were under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed into the Northeast Arkansas during the evening of December 5th, 2013. Widespread rain was already occurring, due to the combination of abundant mid-level moisture, an enhanced upper level jet and an upper level disturbance moving through the area. As a result of the cold front, temperatures plummeted and turned rain over to freezing rain and sleet across Eastern Arkansas. The freezing line stalled in its progression to the southeast around midnight and remained roughly located from Forrest City to West Memphis. After sunrise...the freezing line pushed southeast and brought arctic air into the rest of East-Central Arkansas. Meanwhile...freezing rain and sleet changed over to snow across Northeast Arkansas during the morning of December 6th, 2013. The wintry precipitation tapered off during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 6th, 2013. Total snow amounts ranged from an inch around Jonesboro, to as much as 8 inches or more across Randolph and Lawrence Counties. Just southeast of the heavy snow accumulations, a corridor of heavy sleet occurred. The corridor, including Craighead, Poinsett, Northeast Mississippi and Southeast Greene Counties, received greater than one and a half inches of sleet. In addition, locations northwest of a Forrest City to a Jericho line, received at least a quarter inch of ice. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. Many accidents occurred as a result. A few trees and power lines fell due to the ice as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of a quarter inch of ice, around an inch of sleet, and between 2-6 inches of snow fell across Clay County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Arctic air overspread Arkansas, an area of low pressure aloft moved toward the state from the southwestern United States. As the low pressure approached, moisture moved up over the cold air, resulting in wintry precipitation. In northern Arkansas, freezing rain and sleet began during the morning of the 5th, then gradually changed to sleet, and finally to mainly snow during the wee hours of the 6th. Rain initially fell in central and southern Arkansas on the 5th. On the morning of the 6th, rain changed to freezing rain and sleet. Snow was added to the mix in some places during the afternoon. Late on the morning of the 6th, rain changed over to freezing rain and some sleet in southern Arkansas. All precipitation came to an end in all parts of Arkansas by mid-evening on the 6th. There was one fatality directly related to the winter weather. On the morning of the 6th, a large limb fell into a camping trailer 4 miles north-northwest of Dover in Pope County. The 62-year-old man in the trailer was killed. There was one indirect injury on the 9th when the canopy at a gas station in Ash Flat (Sharp County) collapsed under the weight of ice and snow. Other structural collapses occurred in northern and western Arkansas for several days after the sleet and snow.||Ice storm conditions caused trees and power lines to fall in parts of western Arkansas. About 40,000 electric customers lost power in the Little Rock County Warning Area. Power outages were especially prevalent in Johnson, Pope, Logan, Yell, Polk, Scott, and Montgomery counties. It took six days to restore power to all customers. Arkansas Forestry Commission crews were called out to clear roads in Pope, Logan, Scott, and Polk counties. Most schools and government offices closed due to the winter weather. More than 50 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport. Numerous events leading up to Christmas, such as city parades, the lighting ceremony for the State Capitol and the state high school football playoffs, were canceled or postponed by the winter weather.||President Barack Obama declared a federal disaster in the following counties:  Johnson, Logan, Marion, Newton, Polk, Scott, Searcy, Sharp, and Van Buren.||Total accumulations of snow and sleet reached 8 to 12 inches across much of northwest and north central Arkansas. Accumulations tapered off farther south with only 1 to 2 inches of snow and sleet in parts of west central and central Arkansas.||Freezing rain accruals occurred over the northern three-quarters of the state. Most totals were in the .10 to .50 inch range, but parts of west central Arkansas experienced an ice storm, with accruals exceeding .75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totaled 9 inches at Harrison (cooperative observer) and Lead Hill (cooperative observer), and 6.5 inches 4 miles northeast of Omaha (cooperative observer). Due to the weight of accumulated ice and snow, carports and barns collapsed, roofs of the old federal building and a building at the airport were damaged in Harrison, and some residences had ice dams on the roof, resulting in leaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, morning thunderstorms in the northeast part of Arkansas sent out an outflow boundary of cool air. As this boundary collided with hot, humid air over central Arkansas at midday, new storms developed very quickly and a few of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain gage operated by the Little Rock Wastewater Utility measured 1.16 inches of rain in 34 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, morning thunderstorms in the northeast part of Arkansas sent out an outflow boundary of cool air. As this boundary collided with hot, humid air over central Arkansas at midday, new storms developed very quickly and a few of the storms became severe.

A large blocking ridge of high pressure was centered well north and west of the region with thunderstorms developing with the aid of afternoon heating along the periphery of the ridge. The atmosphere became very unstable during the afternoon of July 11th and once these storms developed, additional thunderstorms developed along residual boundaries from previous storms. Damaging wind gusts were the main threat from these thunderstorms but large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed in Center Point, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed into the Northeast Arkansas during the evening of December 5th, 2013. Widespread rain was already occurring, due to the combination of abundant mid-level moisture, an enhanced upper level jet and an upper level disturbance moving through the area. As a result of the cold front, temperatures plummeted and turned rain over to freezing rain and sleet across Eastern Arkansas. The freezing line stalled in its progression to the southeast around midnight and remained roughly located from Forrest City to West Memphis. After sunrise...the freezing line pushed southeast and brought arctic air into the rest of East-Central Arkansas. Meanwhile...freezing rain and sleet changed over to snow across Northeast Arkansas during the morning of December 6th, 2013. The wintry precipitation tapered off during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 6th, 2013. Total snow amounts ranged from an inch around Jonesboro, to as much as 8 inches or more across Randolph and Lawrence Counties. Just southeast of the heavy snow accumulations, a corridor of heavy sleet occurred. The corridor, including Craighead, Poinsett, Northeast Mississippi and Southeast Greene Counties, received greater than one and a half inches of sleet. In addition, locations northwest of a Forrest City to a Jericho line, received at least a quarter inch of ice. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. Many accidents occurred as a result. A few trees and power lines fell due to the ice as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of a quarter of an inch of ice, between 1 to 2 inches of sleet, and up to an inch of snow fell across Craighead County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 14th, a front had stalled to the southeast of Arkansas. As an area of low pressure traveled along the front, moisture was thrown back into the cold air over Arkansas. The result was light freezing rain and some sleet in parts of south central and southeast Arkansas during the morning and afternoon. Icy patches developed on roadways, primarily on elevated surfaces.

A cold front moved into northwest Arkansas on the morning of the 20th and lingered in the state until the night of the 21st. While the front was making its way slowly across the state, an area of low pressure arrived from the southwest.  The result was heavy rain and flash flooding in the cooler air behind the front. In the warmer air ahead of the front, isolated tornadoes occurred.||Some of the larger rainfall totals for the 48 hours that included the 20th and 21st were:  7.92 inches 3.3 miles west of Carlisle (Lonoke Co.), 7.68 inches at Little Dixie (Woodruff Co.), 7.66 inches at Des Arc (Prairie Co.), 7.37 inches at Keo (Lonoke Co.), 6.71 inches at Georgetown (White Co.), 6.50 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 6.45 inches at Augusta (Woodruff Co.), 6.29 inches at Chenal Valley (Pulaski County), 6.15 inches at DeGray Lake (Clark Co.), 6.12 inches 8.8 miles west of Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), and 6.00 inches 0.5 mile southwest of Beebe (White Co.).  Numerous other stations reported amounts between 4 and 6 inches, especially in eastern, central, and southern parts of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water entered a business in Newport and a half-mile section of Arkansas 384 flooded in Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Arctic air overspread Arkansas, an area of low pressure aloft moved toward the state from the southwestern United States. As the low pressure approached, moisture moved up over the cold air, resulting in wintry precipitation. In northern Arkansas, freezing rain and sleet began during the morning of the 5th, then gradually changed to sleet, and finally to mainly snow during the wee hours of the 6th. Rain initially fell in central and southern Arkansas on the 5th. On the morning of the 6th, rain changed to freezing rain and sleet. Snow was added to the mix in some places during the afternoon. Late on the morning of the 6th, rain changed over to freezing rain and some sleet in southern Arkansas. All precipitation came to an end in all parts of Arkansas by mid-evening on the 6th. There was one fatality directly related to the winter weather. On the morning of the 6th, a large limb fell into a camping trailer 4 miles north-northwest of Dover in Pope County. The 62-year-old man in the trailer was killed. There was one indirect injury on the 9th when the canopy at a gas station in Ash Flat (Sharp County) collapsed under the weight of ice and snow. Other structural collapses occurred in northern and western Arkansas for several days after the sleet and snow.||Ice storm conditions caused trees and power lines to fall in parts of western Arkansas. About 40,000 electric customers lost power in the Little Rock County Warning Area. Power outages were especially prevalent in Johnson, Pope, Logan, Yell, Polk, Scott, and Montgomery counties. It took six days to restore power to all customers. Arkansas Forestry Commission crews were called out to clear roads in Pope, Logan, Scott, and Polk counties. Most schools and government offices closed due to the winter weather. More than 50 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport. Numerous events leading up to Christmas, such as city parades, the lighting ceremony for the State Capitol and the state high school football playoffs, were canceled or postponed by the winter weather.||President Barack Obama declared a federal disaster in the following counties:  Johnson, Logan, Marion, Newton, Polk, Scott, Searcy, Sharp, and Van Buren.||Total accumulations of snow and sleet reached 8 to 12 inches across much of northwest and north central Arkansas. Accumulations tapered off farther south with only 1 to 2 inches of snow and sleet in parts of west central and central Arkansas.||Freezing rain accruals occurred over the northern three-quarters of the state. Most totals were in the .10 to .50 inch range, but parts of west central Arkansas experienced an ice storm, with accruals exceeding .75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totaled 12 inches at Mount Sherman (law enforcement), 9 inches at Jasper (law enforcement), 8 inches at Parthenon (cooperative observer), and 6.5 inches 2.7 miles west-northwest of Jasper (CoCoRaHS).

A strong cold front moved through the southern half of Arkansas during the evening hours of January 13th and through the overnight hours of January 14th.  Rain and a few storms accompanied the passage of this cold front across the southern half of the state.  A very cold and shallow airmass followed the cold front yet southwesterly winds overran this cold shallow airmass above five thousand feet in the atmosphere. Meanwhile, weak disturbances moved towards the state from the west and with plenty of moisture still in place, overrunning precipitation developed during the morning of January 14th.  With surface temperatures near or slightly below freezing, the precipitation fell as a mixture of sleet with some freezing rain noted as well. Accumulations were very light, mainly less than a tenth of an inch for the event. Regardless, bridges and overpasses quickly became slick resulting in several automobile accidents across mainly Nevada, Lafayette, Columbia and Union Counties in Southwest Arkansas.  In addition, there were some minor power outages from falling limbs due to the weight of the ice.

Freezing rain and sleet moved into southern Arkansas late in the day on the 7th and spread northward, reaching northern Arkansas during the wee hours of the 8th. Precipitation tapered off after sunrise on the 8th, with only patchy freezing drizzle and freezing fog lingering through the remainder of the morning. The wintry precipitation caused hazardous driving conditions.

There was one fatality directly related to the winter weather. On the morning of the 6th, a large limb fell into a camping trailer 4 miles north-northwest of Dover in Pope County. The 62-year-old man in the trailer was killed. There was one indirect injury on the 9th when the canopy at a gas station in Ash Flat (Sharp County) collapsed under the weight of ice and snow. Other structural collapses occurred in northern and western Arkansas for several days after the sleet and snow.||Ice storm conditions caused trees and power lines to fall in parts of western Arkansas. Most schools and government offices closed due to the winter weather. More than 50 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system and associated cold front triggered thunderstorms in Arkansas, beginning on the evening of the 20th and continuing for much of the day on the 21st. A few of the storms became severe, while others produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were under water south of Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large blocking ridge of high pressure was centered well north and west of the region with thunderstorms developing with the aid of afternoon heating along the periphery of the ridge. The atmosphere became very unstable during the afternoon of July 11th and once these storms developed, additional thunderstorms developed along residual boundaries from previous storms. Damaging wind gusts were the main threat from these thunderstorms but large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 300 foot section of a roof from a chicken house was torn off on Boyd Road in the Center Point community.  Trees were also downed on several county roads throughout Howard County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the southern half of Arkansas during the evening hours of January 13th and through the overnight hours of January 14th.  Rain and a few storms accompanied the passage of this cold front across the southern half of the state.  A very cold and shallow airmass followed the cold front yet southwesterly winds overran this cold shallow airmass above five thousand feet in the atmosphere. Meanwhile, weak disturbances moved towards the state from the west and with plenty of moisture still in place, overrunning precipitation developed during the morning of January 14th.  With surface temperatures near or slightly below freezing, the precipitation fell as a mixture of sleet with some freezing rain noted as well. Accumulations were very light, mainly less than a tenth of an inch for the event. Regardless, bridges and overpasses quickly became slick resulting in several automobile accidents across mainly Nevada, Lafayette, Columbia and Union Counties in Southwest Arkansas.  In addition, there were some minor power outages from falling limbs due to the weight of the ice.

Freezing rain and sleet moved into southern Arkansas late in the day on the 7th and spread northward, reaching northern Arkansas during the wee hours of the 8th. Precipitation tapered off after sunrise on the 8th, with only patchy freezing drizzle and freezing fog lingering through the remainder of the morning. The wintry precipitation caused hazardous driving conditions.

Late on the morning of the 6th, rain changed over to freezing rain and some sleet in southern Arkansas. All precipitation came to an end in all parts of Arkansas by mid-evening on the 6th. There was one fatality directly related to the winter weather. On the morning of the 6th, a large limb fell into a camping trailer 4 miles north-northwest of Dover in Pope County. The 62-year-old man in the trailer was killed. There was one indirect injury on the 9th when the canopy at a gas station in Ash Flat (Sharp County) collapsed under the weight of ice and snow. Other structural collapses occurred in northern and western Arkansas for several days after the sleet and snow.||Ice storm conditions caused trees and power lines to fall in parts of western Arkansas. About 40,000 electric customers lost power in the Little Rock County Warning Area. Power outages were especially prevalent in Johnson, Pope, Logan, Yell, Polk, Scott, and Montgomery counties. It took six days to restore power to all customers. Arkansas Forestry Commission crews were called out to clear roads in Pope, Logan, Scott, and Polk counties. Most schools and government offices closed due to the winter weather. More than 50 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport. Numerous events leading up to Christmas, such as city parades, the lighting ceremony for the State Capitol and the state high school football playoffs, were canceled or postponed by the winter weather.||President Barack Obama declared a federal disaster in the following counties:  Johnson, Logan, Marion, Newton, Polk, Scott, Searcy, Sharp, and Van Buren.||Total accumulations of snow and sleet reached 8 to 12 inches across much of northwest and north central Arkansas. Accumulations tapered off farther south with only 1 to 2 inches of snow and sleet in parts of west central and central Arkansas.||Freezing rain accruals occurred over the northern three-quarters of the state. Most totals were in the .10 to .50 inch range, but parts of west central Arkansas experienced an ice storm, with accruals exceeding .75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and snow totaled 2.2 inches at Searcy (cooperative observer) and 2 inches at Steprock (cooperative observer) and Pangburn (social media).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system and associated cold front triggered thunderstorms in Arkansas, beginning on the evening of the 20th and continuing for much of the day on the 21st. A few of the storms became severe, while others produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system and associated cold front triggered thunderstorms in Arkansas, beginning on the evening of the 20th and continuing for much of the day on the 21st. A few of the storms became severe, while others produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of large trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northwest Arkansas on the morning of the 20th and lingered in the state until the night of the 21st. While the front was making its way slowly across the state, an area of low pressure arrived from the southwest.  The result was heavy rain and flash flooding in the cooler air behind the front. In the warmer air ahead of the front, isolated tornadoes occurred.||Some of the larger rainfall totals for the 48 hours that included the 20th and 21st were:  7.92 inches 3.3 miles west of Carlisle (Lonoke Co.), 7.68 inches at Little Dixie (Woodruff Co.), 7.66 inches at Des Arc (Prairie Co.), 7.37 inches at Keo (Lonoke Co.), 6.71 inches at Georgetown (White Co.), 6.50 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 6.45 inches at Augusta (Woodruff Co.), 6.29 inches at Chenal Valley (Pulaski County), 6.15 inches at DeGray Lake (Clark Co.), 6.12 inches 8.8 miles west of Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), and 6.00 inches 0.5 mile southwest of Beebe (White Co.).  Numerous other stations reported amounts between 4 and 6 inches, especially in eastern, central, and southern parts of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas 18 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the southern half of Arkansas during the evening hours of January 13th and the predawn hours of January 14th.  Rain and a few storms accompanied the passage of this cold front across the southern half of the state.  A very cold and shallow airmass followed the cold front yet southwesterly winds overran this cold shallow airmass above five thousand feet in the atmosphere.  Meanwhile, weak disturbances moved towards the state from the west and with plenty of moisture still in place, overrunning precipitation developed during the morning of January 15th. With surface temperatures near or slightly below freezing, the precipitation fell as a mixture of sleet with some freezing rain noted as well. Accumulations were very light, mainly less than a tenth of an inch for the event.  Regardless, bridges and overpasses quickly became slick resulting in numerous automobile accidents across the southern half of the state.  In addition, there were some minor power outages from falling limbs due to the weight of the ice.

Freezing rain and sleet moved into southern Arkansas late in the day on the 7th and spread northward, reaching northern Arkansas during the wee hours of the 8th. Precipitation tapered off after sunrise on the 8th, with only patchy freezing drizzle and freezing fog lingering through the remainder of the morning. The wintry precipitation caused hazardous driving conditions.

After Arctic air overspread Arkansas, an area of low pressure aloft moved toward the state from the southwestern United States. As the low pressure approached, moisture moved up over the cold air, resulting in wintry precipitation. In northern Arkansas, freezing rain and sleet began during the morning of the 5th, then gradually changed to sleet, and finally to mainly snow during the wee hours of the 6th. Rain initially fell in central and southern Arkansas on the 5th. On the morning of the 6th, rain changed to freezing rain and sleet. Snow was added to the mix in some places during the afternoon. Late on the morning of the 6th, rain changed over to freezing rain and some sleet in southern Arkansas. All precipitation came to an end in all parts of Arkansas by mid-evening on the 6th. There was one fatality directly related to the winter weather. On the morning of the 6th, a large limb fell into a camping trailer 4 miles north-northwest of Dover in Pope County. The 62-year-old man in the trailer was killed. There was one indirect injury on the 9th when the canopy at a gas station in Ash Flat (Sharp County) collapsed under the weight of ice and snow. Other structural collapses occurred in northern and western Arkansas for several days after the sleet and snow.||Ice storm conditions caused trees and power lines to fall in parts of western Arkansas. About 40,000 electric customers lost power in the Little Rock County Warning Area. Power outages were especially prevalent in Johnson, Pope, Logan, Yell, Polk, Scott, and Montgomery counties. Most schools and government offices closed due to the winter weather. More than 50 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large blocking ridge of high pressure was centered well north and west of the region with thunderstorms developing with the aid of afternoon heating along the periphery of the ridge. The atmosphere became very unstable during the afternoon of July 11th and once these storms developed, additional thunderstorms developed along residual boundaries from previous storms. Damaging wind gusts were the main threat from these thunderstorms but large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven trees were downed on the west side of Dequeen, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northwest Arkansas on the morning of the 20th and lingered in the state until the night of the 21st. While the front was making its way slowly across the state, an area of low pressure arrived from the southwest.  The result was heavy rain and flash flooding in the cooler air behind the front. In the warmer air ahead of the front, isolated tornadoes occurred.||Some of the larger rainfall totals for the 48 hours that included the 20th and 21st were:  7.92 inches 3.3 miles west of Carlisle (Lonoke Co.), 7.68 inches at Little Dixie (Woodruff Co.), 7.66 inches at Des Arc (Prairie Co.), 7.37 inches at Keo (Lonoke Co.), 6.71 inches at Georgetown (White Co.), 6.50 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 6.45 inches at Augusta (Woodruff Co.), 6.29 inches at Chenal Valley (Pulaski County), 6.15 inches at DeGray Lake (Clark Co.), 6.12 inches 8.8 miles west of Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), and 6.00 inches 0.5 mile southwest of Beebe (White Co.).  Numerous other stations reported amounts between 4 and 6 inches, especially in eastern, central, and southern parts of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas 89 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the southern half of Arkansas during the evening hours of January 13th and the predawn hours of January 14th.  Rain and a few storms accompanied the passage of this cold front across the southern half of the state.  A very cold and shallow airmass followed the cold front yet southwesterly winds overran this cold shallow airmass above five thousand feet in the atmosphere.  Meanwhile, weak disturbances moved towards the state from the west and with plenty of moisture still in place, overrunning precipitation developed during the morning of January 15th. With surface temperatures near or slightly below freezing, the precipitation fell as a mixture of sleet with some freezing rain noted as well. Accumulations were very light, mainly less than a tenth of an inch for the event.  Regardless, bridges and overpasses quickly became slick resulting in numerous automobile accidents across the southern half of the state.  In addition, there were some minor power outages from falling limbs due to the weight of the ice.

The arrival of a cold front triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 12th through the morning of the 13th. The front stalled for awhile, causing heavy rain to develop near the front. In a swath from west central Arkansas through the Arkansas River Valley into north central and northeast sections of the state, rainfall amounts of 3.5 to 6 inches were common, resulting in flash flooding. Some of the larger rainfall totals included 6.02 inches at Dardanelle (Yell Co.), 5.39 inches at Calamine (Sharp Co.), 5.20 at Russellville (Pope Co.), 5.10 at Mountain View (Stone Co.), 4.98 at Clinton (Van Buren Co.), 4.75 at Damascus (Van Buren Co.), 4.70 at Batesville Lock and Dam (Independence Co.), 4.15 at Center Ridge (Conway Co.), 4.00 at Hardy (Sharp Co.), 3.90 at Melbourne (Izard Co.) and Salem (Fulton Co.), 3.88 at Evening Shade (Sharp Co.), and 3.70 at Mammoth Spring (Sharp Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county roads and some highways were impassable or had water over them, including some areas around Shirley, Choctaw, Scotland, Damascus, the Van Buren/Stone County line, and Fairfield Bay. The county declared a disaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the 29th, and a few of the storms became severe. There was spotty wind damage and a couple of weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado blew part of the roof off a mobile home and blew some shingles off several houses. A few trees were blown down, one of which fell on power lines and snapped a power pole. One carport was blown down and another one was blown into a tree line. Several outbuildings were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the 29th, and a few of the storms became severe. There was spotty wind damage and a couple of weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado blew down a few trees and took part of the roof off an old barn. This same barn lost part of its roof in a tornado on April 25, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures, a moist atmosphere, and the arrival of a weak area of low pressure aloft triggered an isolated severe storm in western Arkansas on the afternoon of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down around Centerville, knocking out power to about 1,000 electric customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued dry weather led to the development of severe drought over parts of southeastern Arkansas after the middle of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over much of the southern half of Cleveland County after the middle of August. By the end of the month, severe drought covered all but the northwestern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During August, afternoon heating and the passage of a couple of cold fronts produced only spotty rainfall across southern Arkansas. By the end of the month, severe drought began to appear across much of the southern part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed in the southeastern panhandle portion of Jefferson County late in August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During August, afternoon heating and the passage of a couple of cold fronts produced only spotty rainfall across southern Arkansas. By the end of the month, severe drought began to appear across much of the southern part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over the southern half of Arkansas County late in August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over all of Calhoun County late in August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over the southeastern corner of Dallas County late in August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over the southeastern two-thirds of Ouachita County late in August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Triple digit temperatures and dewpoints that mixed down into the lower 60s during the afternoon hours resulted in ample downburst conditions across Southwest Arkansas and Northeast Texas once storms developed from a disturbance aloft. These storms propagated southward and downed trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed just north of De Queen which downed powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a cold front, hot weather, and a humid atmosphere helped to trigger some severe thunderstorms in western and central Arkansas during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northwest Arkansas on the morning of the 20th and lingered in the state until the night of the 21st. While the front was making its way slowly across the state, an area of low pressure arrived from the southwest.  The result was heavy rain and flash flooding in the cooler air behind the front. In the warmer air ahead of the front, isolated tornadoes occurred.||Some of the larger rainfall totals for the 48 hours that included the 20th and 21st were:  7.92 inches 3.3 miles west of Carlisle (Lonoke Co.), 7.68 inches at Little Dixie (Woodruff Co.), 7.66 inches at Des Arc (Prairie Co.), 7.37 inches at Keo (Lonoke Co.), 6.71 inches at Georgetown (White Co.), 6.50 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 6.45 inches at Augusta (Woodruff Co.), 6.29 inches at Chenal Valley (Pulaski County), 6.15 inches at DeGray Lake (Clark Co.), 6.12 inches 8.8 miles west of Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), and 6.00 inches 0.5 mile southwest of Beebe (White Co.).  Numerous other stations reported amounts between 4 and 6 inches, especially in eastern, central, and southern parts of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas 323 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a cold front triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 12th through the morning of the 13th. The front stalled for awhile, causing heavy rain to develop near the front. In a swath from west central Arkansas through the Arkansas River Valley into north central and northeast sections of the state, rainfall amounts of 3.5 to 6 inches were common, resulting in flash flooding. Some of the larger rainfall totals included 6.02 inches at Dardanelle (Yell Co.), 5.39 inches at Calamine (Sharp Co.), 5.20 at Russellville (Pope Co.), 5.10 at Mountain View (Stone Co.), 4.98 at Clinton (Van Buren Co.), 4.75 at Damascus (Van Buren Co.), 4.70 at Batesville Lock and Dam (Independence Co.), 4.15 at Center Ridge (Conway Co.), 4.00 at Hardy (Sharp Co.), 3.90 at Melbourne (Izard Co.) and Salem (Fulton Co.), 3.88 at Evening Shade (Sharp Co.), and 3.70 at Mammoth Spring (Sharp Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximate 98% of the county roads had wash-outs on them. Water was still over many roads on the 15th, with three roads still closed. The county declared a disaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Arctic air overspread Arkansas, an area of low pressure aloft moved toward the state from the southwestern United States. As the low pressure approached, moisture moved up over the cold air, resulting in wintry precipitation. In northern Arkansas, freezing rain and sleet began during the morning of the 5th, then gradually changed to sleet, and finally to mainly snow during the wee hours of the 6th. Rain initially fell in central and southern Arkansas on the 5th. On the morning of the 6th, rain changed to freezing rain and sleet. Snow was added to the mix in some places during the afternoon. Late on the morning of the 6th, rain changed over to freezing rain and some sleet in southern Arkansas. All precipitation came to an end in all parts of Arkansas by mid-evening on the 6th. There was one fatality directly related to the winter weather. On the morning of the 6th, a large limb fell into a camping trailer 4 miles north-northwest of Dover in Pope County. The 62-year-old man in the trailer was killed. There was one indirect injury on the 9th when the canopy at a gas station in Ash Flat (Sharp County) collapsed under the weight of ice and snow. Other structural collapses occurred in northern and western Arkansas for several days after the sleet and snow.||Ice storm conditions caused trees and power lines to fall in parts of western Arkansas. About 40,000 electric customers lost power in the Little Rock County Warning Area. Power outages were especially prevalent in Johnson, Pope, Logan, Yell, Polk, Scott, and Montgomery counties. It took six days to restore power to all customers. Arkansas Forestry Commission crews were called out to clear roads in Pope, Logan, Scott, and Polk counties. Most schools and government offices closed due to the winter weather. More than 50 flights were canceled at the Little Rock airport. Numerous events leading up to Christmas, such as city parades, the lighting ceremony for the State Capitol and the state high school football playoffs, were canceled or postponed by the winter weather.||President Barack Obama declared a federal disaster in the following counties:  Johnson, Logan, Marion, Newton, Polk, Scott, Searcy, Sharp, and Van Buren.||Total accumulations of snow and sleet reached 8 to 12 inches across much of northwest and north central Arkansas. Accumulations tapered off farther south with only 1 to 2 inches of snow and sleet in parts of west central and central Arkansas.||Freezing rain accruals occurred over the northern three-quarters of the state. Most totals were in the .10 to .50 inch range, but parts of west central Arkansas experienced an ice storm, with accruals exceeding .75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet totaled 10.5 inches 7.3 miles northwest of Yellville (CoCoRaHS), 10 inches at Oakland (social media), 9 inches 3.1 miles south-southeast of Bruno (CoCoRaHS), and 7 inches at Yellville (law enforcement). In Flippin, a mechanic shop and church collapsed from the weight of accumulated ice and snow. Elsewhere in the county, collapses also occurred at an auto sales/repair shop, a large sawmill, and an auction/storage center. About a dozen turkey houses collapsed, killing tens of thousands of birds. All three school districts in the county had damage due to collapsed awnings and ice dams causing roof leaks. Close to 100 businesses and residences also had roof leaks due to ice dams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed into the Northeast Arkansas during the evening of December 5th, 2013. Widespread rain was already occurring, due to the combination of abundant mid-level moisture, an enhanced upper level jet and an upper level disturbance moving through the area. As a result of the cold front, temperatures plummeted and turned rain over to freezing rain and sleet across Eastern Arkansas. The freezing line stalled in its progression to the southeast around midnight and remained roughly located from Forrest City to West Memphis. After sunrise...the freezing line pushed southeast and brought arctic air into the rest of East-Central Arkansas. Meanwhile...freezing rain and sleet changed over to snow across Northeast Arkansas during the morning of December 6th, 2013. The wintry precipitation tapered off during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 6th, 2013. Total snow amounts ranged from an inch around Jonesboro, to as much as 8 inches or more across Randolph and Lawrence Counties. Just southeast of the heavy snow accumulations, a corridor of heavy sleet occurred. The corridor, including Craighead, Poinsett, Northeast Mississippi and Southeast Greene Counties, received greater than one and a half inches of sleet. In addition, locations northwest of a Forrest City to a Jericho line, received at least a quarter inch of ice. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. Many accidents occurred as a result. A few trees and power lines fell due to the ice as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of a quarter inch of ice and between 1 and 2 inches of sleet fell across Poinsett County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved out of Chicot County into Desha County. One mobile home was knocked off its foundation, had its porch torn off, and had a large metal intermodal shipping container in the back yard overturn and crash into the rear wall of the mobile home, smashing in the kitchen wall. A house had its porch torn off, roof damage, a hole punched in a wall, and broken windows. Another house had a considerable amount of roofing material torn off. At another house, the attached carport was torn off, with much of it landing on the roof of the house. Several sheds were destroyed, large trees were blown down, and several power poles were snapped off. The tornado then exited Desha County 5.1 miles east-northeast of Halley and re-entered Chicot County near the Yellow Bend Port.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over northwestern and west central Arkansas during the late afternoon hours of the 23rd. These storms persisted over the region well into the early morning hours of the 24th. The strongest storms produced strong outflow wind. Lightning from these storms also caused a structural fire in Bentonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home, resulting in a fire that destroyed it and damaged the adjacent house.

Constuction in this portion of Johnson may have been a factor in the flooding.

Just southeast of the heavy snow accumulations, a corridor of heavy sleet occurred. The corridor, including Craighead, Poinsett, Northeast Mississippi and Southeast Greene Counties, received greater than one and a half inches of sleet. In addition, locations northwest of a Forrest City to a Jericho line, received at least a quarter inch of ice. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. Many accidents occurred as a result. A few trees and power lines fell due to the ice as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of a quarter inch of ice and around a half inch of sleet fell across St. Francis County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon heating resulted in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Southwest Arkansas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed in the south part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the morning and afternoon of July 26th, ahead of a weak surface front and associated upper level disturbance which moved across the region.  Surface low pressure developed during the early evening hours along the surface front, and enhanced the development of stronger convection across a warm and moderately unstable region in Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas. These storms quickly became severe and produced damaging winds which downed trees and power lines across this region before diminishing over extreme North Central Louisiana by late evening. A secondary cluster of severe thunderstorms developed over Northwest Louisiana near this surface low and front, which surged southeast across West Central Louisiana during the late evening and early morning hours of the 26th-27th. Damaging winds again downed trees and power lines across these areas, before weakening prior to sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed over Hwy. 26 as well as Pump Springs Road north of Nashville, Arkansas.

An arctic cold front pushed into the Northeast Arkansas during the evening of December 5th, 2013. Widespread rain was already occurring, due to the combination of abundant mid-level moisture, an enhanced upper level jet and an upper level disturbance moving through the area. As a result of the cold front, temperatures plummeted and turned rain over to freezing rain and sleet across Eastern Arkansas. The freezing line stalled in its progression to the southeast around midnight and remained roughly located from Forrest City to West Memphis. After sunrise...the freezing line pushed southeast and brought arctic air into the rest of East-Central Arkansas. Meanwhile...freezing rain and sleet changed over to snow across Northeast Arkansas during the morning of December 6th, 2013. The wintry precipitation tapered off during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 6th, 2013. Total snow amounts ranged from an inch around Jonesboro, to as much as 8 inches or more across Randolph and Lawrence Counties. Just southeast of the heavy snow accumulations, a corridor of heavy sleet occurred. The corridor, including Craighead, Poinsett, Northeast Mississippi and Southeast Greene Counties, received greater than one and a half inches of sleet. In addition, locations northwest of a Forrest City to a Jericho line, received at least a quarter inch of ice. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. Many accidents occurred as a result. A few trees and power lines fell due to the ice as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a quarter of an inch of ice and a half inch of sleet fell across Lee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell well below freezing during the predawn hours of December 31st near and along the Red River in Miller County and across portions of Howard County in Southwest Arkansas.  The sky was mostly clear and with rather wet soil conditions from previous rainfall...perfect conditions developed for the formation of patchy dense freezing fog.  A thin layer of ice formed across elevated bridges and overpasses with numerous accidents and injuries reported on the Hwy. 71 Index bridge near Texarkana.  There was also one indirect fatality reported from an accident on the bridge.  There were two additional injuries reported in Howard County near Nashville when a driver failed to stop at a stop sign due to the low visibility.

A shallow cold layer of air moved out of the Southern Plains and into the Lower Mississippi Valley on the 24th of November.  The flow aloft across the southern portion of the country was from the southwest. This resulted in an overrunning pattern of warm and moist air overrunning the cold shallow subfreezing air. The end result was a period of freezing rain and sleet across portions of Southwest Arkansas.  Ice accumulation was mostly less than one quarter of an inch.  Temperatures were well above normal before this airmass settled into the region so the ice accumulation was mainly on elevated objects such as bridges, overpasses, trees, powerlines and car tops.

A very hot and unstable airmass prevailed across the Mid-South during the afternoon of June 27, 2013. A weak surface boundary was stretched from Northeast Arkansas into North Mississippi.  A few thunderstorms developed along this boundary in Northeast Arkansas during the late afternoon and eventually formed a mesoscale convective system that traveled southeast across the Memphis Metropolitan area into Northwest Mississippi. A few severe storms occurred with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of quarter size hail in the Valley View and Lawson Road areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed over Kansas and Missouri and eventually moved southeast into Eastern Arkansas and the Memphis Metropolitan area during the early morning hours of June 28, 2013. A few storms were severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees along County Road 917 near Armorel. A shed was also destroyed. Wood fences were blown down at six to eight homes in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell well below freezing during the predawn hours of December 31st near and along the Red River in Miller County and across portions of Howard County in Southwest Arkansas.  The sky was mostly clear and with rather wet soil conditions from previous rainfall...perfect conditions developed for the formation of patchy dense freezing fog.  A thin layer of ice formed across elevated bridges and overpasses with numerous accidents and injuries reported on the Hwy. 71 Index bridge near Texarkana.  There was also one indirect fatality reported from an accident on the bridge.  There were two additional injuries reported in Howard County near Nashville when a driver failed to stop at a stop sign due to the low visibility.

An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. Strong winds aloft combined with an increasingly unstable airmass set the stage for severe weather across the region. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked numerous limbs down in Hoxie. One power pole was also pushed partway over. In addition, a small metal building was destroyed and gutters were torn from a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure system moved northeast out of the Texas/Oklahoma Panhandle Region into the Mid-South during the evening of March 21st, 2013. Heavy snow fell across Northeast Arkansas as a result. Snow accumulations ranged from two to seven inches across the area. The snow tapered of during the morning hours of March 22nd, 2013. The snow created hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from two to five inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable airmass developed across the Southern Plains and Lower Mississippi Valley during the day as an ejecting upper trough moved into the Plains. A strong cold front moved out of Southern Oklahoma and North Texas during the day with a very strong pressure gradient ahead of the cold front. Strong gradient winds from the south approached 40 mph across many locations with the ambient temperature reaching 70 degrees. Deep layer shear in the lowest 6 kilometers were in excess of 50kts with strong low level shear noted as well. Combine that with excessive atmospheric lapse rates and all the ingredients were present for strong to severe thunderstorms that produced large hail, damaging wind gusts and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell north of Fouke, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unseasonably moist airmass dominated the Southern Plains into the Lower Mississippi Valley on the 29th of January.  Daytime high temperatures were well into the 70s with dewpoint temperatures into the 60s across the entire region.  Strong gradient winds were prevalent from the south during the day with wind gusts approaching 40 mph across a few locations.  A vigorous upper level storm system dove out of the Central and Southern Plains and moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the afternoon and overnight hours of the 29th.  A 70 knot low level jet preceded the storm development in a very unstable airmass across the region.  In addition, strong low level shear resulted in the development of discreet storms developing ahead of a squall line which produced wide spread wind damage across the region.  While no tornadoes were produced across the Four State Region, strong tornadoes developed across portions of Northern Arkansas and extreme Northeast Louisiana with this storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large barn was destroyed and numerous trees were downed across the area.  A trailer was overturned as well near CR. 413 off of CR. 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall during the month of January improved drought conditions across Randolph County. As a result...only abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained across the area by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to abnormally dry (D0) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough ejected out of the Four Corners region of the country and into the plains during the afternoon hours of February 21st. During the morning of the 21st...elevated convection became rather widespread across the region and most of these storms produced hail. A warm front was located across the Southeast Texas and Southern Louisiana coast during the early morning hours of the 21st but as the day progressed...the warm front came north to near the Interstate 20 corridor of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana. This lead to a very warm and unstable airmass infiltrating the region from the south. Low level shear was very strong both in speed and direction along the vicinity of this warm front and as as result...a few tornadoes developed across the region. Otherwise...the storms continued to produce hail and some wind damage from straight line winds.

A warm and unstable airmass developed across the region during the afternoon hours of February 25th.  This airmass change was in response to a vigorous storm system that moved into the Southern Plains.  A sharp...well defined dryline developed across North Central Texas during the morning hours and moved east during the day.  This dryline acted as a focusing mechanism for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across the region...aided by the lift induced form the upper level storm system.  The storms primarily produced large hail as they moved east across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell in Nashville, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to improve across Howard, Little River and Sevier Counties in Southwest Arkansas.  Rainfall during the month totaled between 2 and 4 inches with isolated higher amounts. Even with the rainfall, Howard, Little River and Sevier Counties remained in D2 Severe Drought classification through the end of the month.

A strong storm system moved from the southern Rockies into the Southern Plains on the 20th and 21st. Snow began during the morning of the 20th across the region and some heavy snow fell before it gradually transitioned to sleet and freezing rain during the evening of the 20th and early morning hours of the 21st. Freezing rain and sleet continued during the morning of the 21st with some ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch. Some power outages occurred as a result of the ice in northwestern Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms formed along a residual cold pool boundary that originated itself north to south across Southern Arkansas during the early morning hours of March 10th.  Surface base instability was non existent during the morning but enough momentum with the eastward propagating line of storms resulted in some wind damage across portions of Miller County, Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds resulted in reports of damage to an airplane hanger and roof damage to a few homes in the Garland community.  A few trees were also downed and the top of a grain silo was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracked from the Southern Plains into the Mid-Mississippi River Valley during the late afternoon into the evening hours of February 18th, 2013. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the disturbance over Northeast Arkansas. Damaging winds was produced from some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew a roof off a house in Lynn. Several trees were uprooted in the area as well.

The difference in pressure between low pressure to the northeast of Arkansas and high pressure to the southwest of the state caused very windy conditions from the morning of the 24th into the evening hours. Many thousands of people lost electricity, but most of the outages were repaired by the middle of the evening on the 24th. Peak wind gusts at airport stations included 47 mph at Little Rock, 44 mph at Pine Bluff and Stuttgart, and 43 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base.
Heavy rainfall from late February kept parts of the Cache River and White River above flood stage from late February through the beginning of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of heavy rain events towards the end of February caused the White River at Augusta to rise above flood stage during the beginning of March. The river crested at 27.99 feet on March 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought worsened slightly over the southwest part of Pike County soon after the middle of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Soon after the middle of March, severe drought spread into parts of southwest Pike County. Conditions then changed little through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the predawn hours across Eastern Oklahoma and North Central Texas. This line of convection became a broken squall line and moved rapidly from northwest to southeast across the region on Easter Sunday. This line of severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage, along with some spotty hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed at the Columbia County, Lafayette County line at U.S. Hwy. 82.

A very warm and unseasonably moist airmass dominated the Southern Plains into the Lower Mississippi Valley on the 29th of January.  Daytime high temperatures were well into the 70s with dewpoint temperatures into the 60s across the entire region.  Strong gradient winds were prevalent from the south during the day with wind gusts approaching 40 mph across a few locations.  A vigorous upper level storm system dove out of the Central and Southern Plains and moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the afternoon and overnight hours of the 29th.  A 70 knot low level jet preceded the storm development in a very unstable airmass across the region.  In addition, strong low level shear resulted in the development of discreet storms developing ahead of a squall line which produced wide spread wind damage across the region.  While no tornadoes were produced across the Four State Region, strong tornadoes developed across portions of Northern Arkansas and extreme Northeast Louisiana with this storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in Magnolia and just south of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved northeast along a stationary front stretching from Southwest Arkansas into the Ohio Valley. Deep moisture surged north from the Gulf of Mexico resulting in a large area of heavy showers and thunderstorms moving into western sections of the Mid-South during the evening of January 12, 2013. The showers and thunderstorms spread slowly east across the remainder of the Mid-South in advance of a cold front through the afternoon of January 13, 2013. The system dropped up to five inches of rain in isolated locations. The heavy rainfall resulted in some flash flooding and areal flooding across parts of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in Jesup and the intersection of Highway 117 and Highway 115 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over central Oklahoma during the early afternoon hours of the 20th. Very unstable air combined with strong low-level and deep-layer shear resulted in long-lived supercells forming over central Oklahoma and moving eastward into eastern Oklahoma. The storms organized into bow echos and line segments over eastern Oklahoma, from which several other tornadoes developed during the early evening hours. One such tornado moved into northwestern Arkansas dissipating north of Cincinnati in Washington County. Two other tornadoes developed in Benton County. Damaging wind and large hail was also widespread across the region from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and a few power poles were blown down by strong thunderstorm wind.

The upper level low pressure system that brought severe weather and flash flooding to Arkansas on May 30th and 31st moved closer to the state on June 1st, producing another round of severe weather and flash flooding. By the evening of the 1st, a cold front pushed through Arkansas, bringing drier air into the state. For the 3-day period from May 30th through June 1st, rainfall amounts in the 6 to 11 inch range were recorded in Scott, Polk, Montgomery, and Yell Counties, as well as parts of Hot Spring, Clark, Saline, and Pulaski Counties. The largest 3-day total that was reported was 11.69 inches at Big Fork in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Releases from Remmel Dam reached 50,700 cubic feet per second, flooding areas adjacent to the Ouachita River downstream.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from late May into early June caused the Ouachita River at Arkadelphia to rise above flood stage just after midnight on the 2nd. The river remained above flood stage into the 3rd, then fell below flood stage during the morning hours on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of low pressure aloft triggered numerous thunderstorms from the 30th into the early morning hours of the 31st. Tornadoes, severe thunderstorms, and flash flooding occurred. The flooding killed six people, and the tornadoes killed one. The same weather system caused severe storms and flash flooding again on June 1st. For the 24-hour period ending at 7 AM on the 31st, a band of very heavy rain fell from Waldron and Mena to Conway and Little Rock and then on to Newport and Augusta. Within this band, rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were common, and isolated totals exceeded 7 inches. Also on the evening of the 30th, 3.09 inches of rain fell in one hour at Little Rock, breaking the all-time record for the greatest amount of rain in one hour in the city. The previous record was 3.00 inches on May 26, 1955. President Barack Obama declared federal disasters in these counties:  Cleburne, Independence, Montgomery, Polk, Scott, Searcy, Stone, Van Buren, and Woodruff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in late May caused the White River at Augusta to rise back above flood stage during the evening hours on the 31st. This river remained above flood stage into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding on rivers within the White River basin continued into May as a result of heavy springtime rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy springtime rainfall kept the White River at Augusta above flood stage from April into much of May before finally dropping below flood stage during the morning of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued into June from springtime heavy rainfall over the Cache and White River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from late May into early June caused the White River at Augusta to rise above flood stage during the evening hours on May 31st. The river remained above flood stage through the first half of June, then fell below flood stage during the early morning hours on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms associated with an advancing cold front moved across the region. A few storms caused wind damage in the form of blown down trees and power lines. Some of these trees fell on structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tractor trailor was rolled over on Highway 65 southeast of Eudora. A few trees and power lines were blown down in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over central Oklahoma during the early afternoon hours of the 20th. Very unstable air combined with strong low-level and deep-layer shear resulted in long-lived supercells forming over central Oklahoma and moving eastward into eastern Oklahoma. The storms organized into bow echos and line segments over eastern Oklahoma, from which several other tornadoes developed during the early evening hours. One such tornado moved into northwestern Arkansas dissipating north of Cincinnati in Washington County. Two other tornadoes developed in Benton County. Damaging wind and large hail was also widespread across the region from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew down numerous large tree limbs, power poles, and power lines across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of June and the first part of July were dry in Arkansas. Areas of severe drought developed in parts of northwest and southwest Arkansas at the middle of the month. Rain later in the month reduced the two areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed across the southwest corner of Boone County at the middle of July, but rain soon thereafter reduced the drought back to moderate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed across the western part of Searcy County at the middle of July, but rain soon thereafter reduced the drought back to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of June and the first part of July were dry in Arkansas. Areas of severe drought developed in parts of northwest and southwest Arkansas at the middle of the month. Rain later in the month reduced the two areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed across much of Newton County at the middle of July, but rain soon thereafter reduced most of the drought back to moderate. The only part of the county remaining in severe drought at the end of July was the west central portion.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over extreme southern Clark County at the middle of July. However, rain during the latter part of the month reduced the drought back to moderate by the end of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and an associated cold front moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the evening of May 21st, 2013. Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system affecting the Mid-South. Some storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The first round of storms moved through during the morning hours while a second round affected the region during the afternoon into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell about 3 miles northeast of Trumann.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of June and the first part of July were dry in Arkansas. Areas of severe drought developed in parts of northwest and southwest Arkansas at the middle of the month. Rain later in the month reduced the two areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over the western half of Ouachita County at the middle of July, then expanded to encompass then entire county. However, rain late in the month reduced the drought back to moderate countywide by the end of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, morning thunderstorms in the northeast part of Arkansas sent out an outflow boundary of cool air. As this boundary collided with hot, humid air over central Arkansas at midday, new storms developed very quickly and a few of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb fell onto a car in Gulpha Gorge. There was one minor injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall later in the month caused the Cache River at Patterson to rise back above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the latter half of the month caused the Cache River at Patterson to rise above flood stage during the early morning hours on the 24th. This river remained above flood stage into the month of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and occasionally heavy rainfall occurred from the evening of the 9th through the morning of the 10th across parts of northwest Arkansas. Rainfall was sufficient to produce widespread flooding over much of Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of South Fish Hatchery Road were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and occasionally heavy rainfall occurred from the evening of the 9th through the morning of the 10th across parts of northwest Arkansas. Rainfall was sufficient to produce widespread flooding over much of Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Main Street was reported flooded near Collins Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and occasionally heavy rainfall occurred from the evening of the 9th through the morning of the 10th across parts of northwest Arkansas. Rainfall was sufficient to produce widespread flooding over much of Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads across northern Washington County were reported flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and occasionally heavy rainfall occurred from the evening of the 9th through the morning of the 10th across parts of northwest Arkansas. Rainfall was sufficient to produce widespread flooding over much of Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Highway 112 at Wallace Lane was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An mesoscale convective system developed over Southern Missouri and Northern Arkansas and pushed across Northeast Arkansas into West Tennessee during the early morning hours of May 31, 2013.  A few severe storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding. A couple of weak tornadoes also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a power pole on Highway 1 in Cherry Valley and several trees along Highway 163.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a cold front triggered a few severe thunderstorms, primarily over the central third of Arkansas, on the evening of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in the Haskell area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a cold front triggered a few severe thunderstorms, primarily over the central third of Arkansas, on the evening of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured 0.64 inch in only 8 minutes at the Searcy airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved east into northwestern Arkansas during the evening of the 19th. Widespread severe weather occurred over eastern Oklahoma as a result of these storms but they weakened by the time they reached the state line and only resulted in isolated damaging wind in northwestern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and power lines were blown down by strong thunderstorm wind across northwestern Carroll County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through Arkansas on the 20th and 21st. As it did so, it met up with tropical moisture that had streamed in from Pacific Hurricane Manuel. The result was heavy rainfall over much of southern, central, and eastern Arkansas. The heaviest rain fell in a swath about 90 miles side from the southwest corner of Arkansas through Pine Bluff into east central Arkansas. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4.5 inches were common south of a line from Murfreesboro to Little Rock to Searcy to McCrory. In the southern part of the state, this rain fell over areas that were in severe to extreme drought. Rain exited southeast Arkansas on the morning of the 22nd. The heavy rain caused some drought-weakened trees to fall. As a result, several thousand people lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts included 4.26 inches at Pine Bluff and 3.05 inches at Harden Lock and Dam on the Arkansas River.
